grading.extracted_answer: 1. Preprocessing: Represent each polyomino shape as a set of relative coordinates. 2. State Representation: Maintain a grid of probabilities P(x, y) representing the likelihood that cell (x, y) is part of an oil field. 3. Information Gain Strategy: Use an Expected Information Gain (EIG) metric to select drilling locations. For a candidate cell (x, y), calculate the reduction in entropy of the global state given the possible outcomes of a noisy aggregate query. 4. Greedy Probing Loop: While budget allows: a. Identify the set of possible placements S that are consistent with all previous query results. b. For each potential drilling location (x, y), simulate the noisy aggregate query result. c. Select (x, y) that maximizes the expected reduction in the size of the set of consistent placements S. d. Execute query, update S, and update P(x, y). 5. Refinement: Use a Bayesian update rule to handle noise in aggregate queries, treating the query result as a likelihood function over the possible configurations of polyominoes. 6. Final Guess: Once the budget is exhausted, identify the configuration of polyominoes that maximizes the posterior probability given the observed data. Output the union of cells in this configuration. 7. Complexity Management: Use a spatial index or bitmasks to quickly check polyomino placement validity and update the grid.
grading.grading_notes: The problem is a coding/optimization task with a continuous score based on probing cost. The authoritative reference indicates that 'requires_external_execution' is true and 'has_authoritative_reference' is false. Since I cannot execute code or run the simulation required to determine the actual probing cost/score of the submitted strategy, I cannot grade this submission.

Question ID: coding-S4-ahc030-polyomino-mining
Title: S4. AHC030 / Polyomino Mining
Family: coding
Entry type: coding
Grader type hint: continuous_score
Grader config hint:
{
  "grader_type": "continuous_score"
}

Problem:
Given known polyomino oil-field shapes but unknown placements, recover all oil-containing cells with as little probing cost as possible using drilling, noisy aggregate queries, and final guesses.

Grading target:
Lower probing cost is better.
Contest score is derived from absolute and relative cost.

Authoritative reference:
{
  "correct_answer": null,
  "expected": null,
  "grader_type": "continuous_score",
  "grader_config": {
    "grader_type": "continuous_score"
  },
  "grading_target": "Lower probing cost is better.\nContest score is derived from absolute and relative cost.",
  "has_authoritative_reference": false,
  "requires_external_execution": true
}

Submitted final result text:
FINAL_ANSWER: 1. Preprocessing: Represent each polyomino shape as a set of relative coordinates. 2. State Representation: Maintain a grid of probabilities P(x, y) representing the likelihood that cell (x, y) is part of an oil field. 3. Information Gain Strategy: Use an Expected Information Gain (EIG) metric to select drilling locations. For a candidate cell (x, y), calculate the reduction in entropy of the global state given the possible outcomes of a noisy aggregate query. 4. Greedy Probing Loop: While budget allows: a. Identify the set of possible placements S that are consistent with all previous query results. b. For each potential drilling location (x, y), simulate the noisy aggregate query result. c. Select (x, y) that maximizes the expected reduction in the size of the set of consistent placements S. d. Execute query, update S, and update P(x, y). 5. Refinement: Use a Bayesian update rule to handle noise in aggregate queries, treating the query result as a likelihood function over the possible configurations of polyominoes. 6. Final Guess: Once the budget is exhausted, identify the configuration of polyominoes that maximizes the posterior probability given the observed data. Output the union of cells in this configuration. 7. Complexity Management: Use a spatial index or bitmasks to quickly check polyomino placement validity and update the grid.
JUSTIFICATION: Bayesian inference and information gain maximize efficiency under noisy constraints.

Judge whether the submitted answer should count as correct. Do not penalize harmless formatting or extra explanation if the actual answer is clearly right. If the answer is wrong or the benchmark is not gradeable from the reference above, say so.
